Using `selectFrom(LOGINMSG)` fetches all columns from the LOGINMSG table into a record, even though only the salt and password hash columns are needed for verification. Consider using `select(LOGINMSG.SALT, LOGINMSG.PASSWORDHASH)` to explicitly specify only the required columns.

Code fragment:
final String likePhrase = "%" + wildcardQueryString + "%";
Condition condition = CARDS.NAME.like(likePhrase).or(CARDS.IDCARD.like(likePhrase)).or(CARDS.PHONE.like(likePhrase)).or(CARDS.ID.like(likePhrase));
Explanation: Using LIKE with wildcards on both sides ('%...%') for searching across multiple columns is inefficient as it cannot use indexes and performs a full table scan. Consider using a dedicated full-text search engine like Elasticsearch, or database-native full-text search capabilities (e.g., PostgreSQL's tsvector/tsquery or MySQL's FULLTEXT indexes).

Using selectFrom(CARDS) fetches all columns from the CARDS table, which may retrieve unnecessary data and impact performance. Explicitly specify only the columns needed for the query using select(CARDS.ID, CARDS.NAME, ...).from(CARDS).

Using select() without specifying columns fetches all columns from both CARDS and TRANSHISTORY tables, which may retrieve unnecessary data. Explicitly list only the required columns, e.g., select(CARDS.ID, CARDS.NAME, TRANSHISTORY.TIME, ...).

Code fragment: public final TableField<CardsRecord, Float> CREDIT = createField(DSL.name("credit"), org.jooq.impl.SQLDataType.REAL.defaultValue(org.jooq.impl.DSL.field("0", org.jooq.impl.SQLDataType.REAL)), this, "");
Explanation: The 'credit' column uses REAL (floating-point) type which can cause rounding errors when storing monetary or fixed-precision values. Change the column type to DECIMAL or NUMERIC with appropriate precision and scale (e.g., DECIMAL(10,2)) to ensure accurate calculations.

Code fragment: public final TableField<CardsRecord, Float> CREDIT = createField(DSL.name("credit"), org.jooq.impl.SQLDataType.REAL.defaultValue(org.jooq.impl.DSL.field("0", org.jooq.impl.SQLDataType.REAL)), this, "");
Explanation: The 'credit' column has a default value of 0 and is nullable, which means NULL and 0 could both represent 'no credit' leading to ambiguity. Either make the column NOT NULL if 0 is a valid default, or remove the default value and use NULL to represent unknown/missing values.
